data.title: TotalEnergies: A Company In Transition Towards A Green Agenda
data.description:
TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) is a French company fully dedicated to the oil and gas industry. Its main business lines include oil exploration and extraction, primarily offshore, liquefied gas, and LNG. This latter segment encompasses the entire processing and value chain of this fuel, which also includes the midstream and upstream segments. Finally, in addition to the general hydrocarbon business, there is a growing segment dedicated to the production of renewable energy. While this report seeks to provide a comprehensive analysis of the company, I want to draw attention to two points that I consider key in a process that seeks to transition to more sustainable energy.

In this regard, the first change I'd like to mention is the company's quest to increase the share of LNG relative to oil extraction. Second, the longer-term transition to energy generation based on a more sustainable matrix and with a much greater share of green energy.

Regarding the transformation the company wants to undertake, I would like to emphasize, from my perspective, that the changes regarding LNG make sense for the company and adapt it to a reality where environmental regulations are becoming increasingly restrictive, particularly in the case of the European Union. However, regarding renewable energy, the situation is much more complex as it entails a radical change in the company's business model. Transitioning from being an oil & gas company to renewable energy requires a different know-how and expertise than Total has amply demonstrated in recent decades in the fossil fuel segment.

Thesis

TotalEnergies is one of the leading European companies in the oil and gas industry. It currently maintains operations around the world, but primarily with a presence in Europe, Africa, and the now largely neglected South and North America. As can be seen below, just over 70% of its revenue comes from Europe.

It's interesting to note that over the last decade, the company has shifted from a strong focus on oil production to a mixed business, with LNG representing 44% of the company's total revenue in 2024 and oil accounting for 43%. The remaining 13% is accounted for by low-carbon energy production.

From my perspective, the company's guidance regarding the increasing relative weight of LNG production to the detriment of oil is extremely relevant. TotalEnergies expects that by 2030, 50% of its total revenue will come from LNG production and 20% from renewable energy. This, in my view, responds to the European Union's initiatives to ensure a major shift in the continent's energy mix in the coming decades. According to the European Green Deal, launched in 2019, the member countries of the European Economic Community committed to achieving a 50% emissions reduction by 2030. This has entailed a series of measures, regulations, and subsidies by member countries to achieve this goal, which, in my view, is somewhat ambitious.

This quest by Total to increase the weight of LNG relative to oil is closely related to the perspective that natural gas can serve as a bridge in the transition toward a complete restructuring of the global energy matrix. In this regard, I would like to emphasize that the vision of the major developed countries for the world to shift to 100% sustainable energy in the short term has proven to be extremely ambitious. In addition, there is a technical and productive factor, where the change implies longer than expected times. In this scenario, natural gas, which generates far fewer emissions than oil, serves as a great bridge to the goal of a world powered entirely by renewable and sustainable energy.

One of the analyses I find extremely important is the relationship between GDP growth over recent years and the increase in electricity demand. Now, it's worth asking how this increase in global electricity demand is being met. As can be seen, LNG growth has been significantly higher than that of oil. While renewable energies represented a greater increase, it's important to note that the initial level value from which each one starts is much lower compared to LNG. If this trend continues, Total's management's vision for the coming decades is interesting.

I now want to turn to the company's most ambitious plan, which involves an almost complete restructuring over the coming decades. Total expects that in the long term, LNG and oil production combined will represent just 27% of revenue. They estimate the remainder will be distributed between Low-Carbon Molecules and electricity generation, which could represent 50%.

Beyond the fact that the plan not only strikes me as ambitious, while radically changing the company's business model from a conventional oil company to a renewable energy generator, what raises red flags for me is the amount of CAPEX the company plans to allocate to this change of direction.

The project involves a gradual modification of the company, the impact on cash flow can already be seen. While it is true that Total estimates its CAPEX to be $17.8 billion this year, with 34% of that amount allocated to the start-up of new oil and LNG projects, there is an amount that cannot be overlooked. From the company's perspective, approximately 27% of this CAPEX will be allocated to low-carbon energy generation. This percentage represents a total of $4.8 billion, a significant amount that is not distributed to shareholders.

Another point I'd like to highlight is that, relative to the industry, Total's margins are generally lower. It's true that compared to other companies in the S&P 500 or the Euro Stocks 600, the company's margins are very good. However, when measured against industry values, as shown in the SeekingAlpha Profitability segment below, the performance is not as spectacular. In fact, I've previously covered smaller companies that have certainly outperformed.

2Q2025 Results

In this segment, I would like to briefly summarize the company's results reported in Q2 2025 and compare them with those seen in prior periods. Before beginning, I would like to point out that the key financial revenue indicators deteriorated quarter-over-quarter and compared to the same period last year, primarily due to the drop in the international price of fossil fuels, primarily oil.

Regarding the company's reported results, revenues in the second quarter of 2025 were $44.7 billion, a 7% drop compared to the $47.9 billion reported in the previous quarter. If we analyze the trend for the first half of 2025 and compare it with the same period in 2024, we see an 8% drop, virtually the same as on a quarter-by-quarter basis.

A similar trend was seen in Total's EBITDA, which reached $9.7 billion. In the first half of the year, the company's EBITDA reached $20.2 billion. This not only represented an 11% drop compared to the same period in 2024, but when compared to the current EV value, it can be calculated that Total's EV/EBITDA ratio stands at 4.38x. Admittedly, this sounds like a low value, but the recent decline in results, coupled with the plan to modify and invest in alternative energy, could raise orange flags.

I want to now describe that cash from operations (CFFO) showed a value of $6.6 billion in 2Q2025. Compared to the first half of the year, the amount was $13.6 billion, which again represents a decrease compared to the same period last year. If we compare this latest annualized value with the company's market cap, we see that the P/CFFO ratio is around 5.06x, which is an attractive value, but there are companies with much more attractive multiples that are undergoing extremely less ambitious change processes.

Below I share the results of the 2Q2025 earnings release published by the company that stood out the most in financial and economic terms.

Valuation

I now want to move on to a valuation using multiples, using the main European companies in the oil & gas sector as my primary comparables. These peers are found in SeekingAlpha's Peers segment, with the exception of Italy's Eni. For these European companies, I decided to maintain the selection made by Petrobras SA, based on their similar market capitalization, geographic diversification, and similar vertical integration. I deliberately excluded the main American companies, as they show a larger size, a different tax structure, and a greater concentration in North American basins.

The ratios shown in the following table were extracted from SeekingAlpha's own Peer segment. For the analysis, I decided to use PE, EV/EBITDA, P/FCF, and P/BV as valuation ratios. For each ratio, I calculated a target value based on a market capitalization-weighted average, and with that target value, I calculated a target price for each ratio.

Once these values ​​were obtained, and based on a weighted average (the weighting factors of which can be seen in the table itself), I arrived at a valuation of the company. I decided to prioritize EV/EBITDA as a relevant multiple over P/BV because in companies of this size and with so many business lines, the book value can be distorted, affecting this result. From the analysis performed, it can be seen that, compared to its main European peers, Total appears to be trading almost at Fair Value. If we add to this the ambitious reform plan, the HOLD rating I gave the company is somewhat justified.

Risks

Finally, I would like to briefly summarize the potential risks the company could face. Undoubtedly, those tied to the current downward trend in oil prices could generate problems in the short and medium term. The possibility that the new US presidential administration will raise import tariffs, especially considering that China is one of the main victims of these measures, could lead not only to a drop in crude oil demand. What would be far worse would be if the conflict escalated into a trade war between the major economic powers, resulting in a drop in GDP. As we saw at the beginning of the report, energy generation and the increase in demand for LNG and green energy are closely linked to global economic growth.

However, I don't want to go into this point in depth, which I've already discussed with other oil and gas industry companies I've covered, such as Permian Resources and APA Resources, and even in my recent report on YPF and Ecopetrol. What I'd like to highlight is a trend that could mark a path Total could take. Recently, EU countries have allocated increasingly large amounts of money to the development of renewable energy and the growth of these industries. These amounts of money have led to the creation of new companies, but have also been partially allocated to many of the continent's oil companies.

This has led to the initiative for a future of sustainable energy generation by companies that have long been explorers and producers of fossil fuels emerging as a search for this money. The fact that European companies in the oil and gas sector tend to become chasers for EU subsidies poses a potential risk, for me. On the one hand, it's true that revenues increase in the short term because investment projects can be carried out at relatively low costs. But on the other, it creates a dependence on a political incentive that raises doubts about profitability once it is no longer in place. The day countries remove their subsidies, the possibility of projects generating sustainable revenues raises a question mark in my mind.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Still A Hold, But Monthly Dividend And Improvements Are Attractive For Income Investors
data.description:
Introduction

As an income investor, BDCs play an integral part in my investment strategy. Previously shunned by investors as BDCs are considered riskier investments, the last few years the sector (BIZD) seems to have grown on them.

This is evident by the amount of analysts on Seeking Alpha that now tout them. When I started here in 2023, it seemed like I was the only one that found them to be not just for short-term income, but for long-term as well.

One reason is likely due to higher for longer interest rates, which have resulted in some strong performances amongst standouts in the sector. However, with increased macro uncertainty, I've become quite cautious of BDCs in recent months.

But Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC), despite uncertainty, continues to impress with each quarter. In this article, I discuss CSWC's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors looking to own BDCs for income may consider Capital Southwest.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Capital Southwest back in May in an article titled: Latest Quarter Confirms Why This Is A Top Income BDC.

During their Q4 earnings, CSWC saw their bottom line decline quarter-over-quarter and the previous year to $0.56. Partly, this was due to lower overall base rates and a $0.05 related to the exit of their CEO.

Despite this, the BDCs' performance was solid, as they managed to see double-digit growth in their top line from $178.1 million to $204.4 million.

Although the drop in Nll resulted in dividend coverage falling below 100%, I remained confident in the company's ability to sustain their dividend due to their undistributed taxable income.

Since, CSWC has had a good performance relative to the S&P (SP500), who's up over 10% compared to 8.52% for the BDC.

Impressive Q1 Performance

In recent months, I've grown weary of some BDCs' ability to sustain their dividends. And dividend cuts over the past year, projected lower base rates, and recent reports show the potential for further economic weakening.

Despite me considering most BDCs holds due to macro uncertainty and suspected lower base rates, Capital Southwest has continued to impress me amidst uncertainty.

The most recent quarter saw their bottom line climb $0.05 from the previous quarter to $0.61.

Although pre-tax net investment income is down year-over-year on a per share basis from $0.64, in totality this actually increased slightly from $31.1 million to $31.2 million.

While some investors get caught up on the per share basis, I like to see how a BDC's bottom line grew on a total basis, as this tells more of their credit quality.

For comparison, Ares Capital (ARCC) saw their bottom line decline on a per share and total basis. This retracted from $0.62 to $0.49. And in totality, net investment income fell from $386 million to $342 million.

While I view ARCC & CSWC as two of the highest quality BDCs in the sector, CSWC's performance in recent months has moved them in 1st place of my favorite BDCs, long held by Ares Capital.

And this has to do with how management has impressively recognized gains harvested from their equity portfolio, which continues to be a secret weapon.

During Q1, CSWC saw their undistributed taxable income increase from $0.79 to $1 per share due to harvested gains of $27.2 million. Higher interest and dividend income also played a role in their top and bottom line growth.

Over the last two quarters, gains from their equity portfolio have amounted to $41.3 million. This is how the BDC was able to offset lower investments during the quarter, which amounted to $115 million, down from $146.2 million the prior quarter.

The $115 million was invested across 3 new companies and 12 existing ones, bringing their total to 122 companies. Furthermore, leverage, payment-in-kind income, and non-accruals also improved.

Improved Balance Sheet

Capital Southwest already had one of the lowest leverage ratios amongst its peers, well below the peer average of 1.19x. Leverage improved to just 0.82x, down from 0.89x in Q4.

In comparison to some of my favorite BDCs in terms of quality and performance, only Fidus Investment (FDUS) had a lower ratio of 0.80x.

ARCC's stood at 1.02x. Moreover, Capital Southwest has no maturing debt until October of next year, with only $150 million due. And liquidity was plentiful, with $444 million in cash and cash equivalents.

I touched on this in my previous article, but the BDC received final approval for their second SBIC license, which gives them additional access to capital up to $175 million over time.

So, CSWC seems to be in a favorable position to navigate potential headwinds from a weakening economy or to take advantage of growth opportunities that present themselves.

Monthly Dividend Switch

In June, Capital Southwest decided to switch to paying a monthly dividend, making the stock even more attractive for the long term. The company declared a $0.1934 dividend. And with the switch, I wonder if peers will follow in their footsteps.

While I remain cautious on the sector due to economic weakening, CSWC remains in a comfortable position to maintain its dividend as a result of their robust liquidity in the form of spillover.

As previously mentioned, this increased from $0.79 to $1 per share in the latest quarter, giving them some flexibility should the FED lower interest rates in the coming months.

NAV & Non-Accruals

NAV in the quarter saw an $0.11 drop from $16.70 as a result of restricted stock issuance. On an annual basis, the NAV decline was modest, with a $0.01 decline to $16.59.

Another reason CSWC's latest quarter was impressive is the BDC saw sequential improvements in their non-performing loans on a fair value basis.

This is something I had been critical of in previous articles and suggested, with the economy seemingly weakening, this was something investors should keep an eye out for in the coming quarters.

But these improved to 0.8%, down from 1.7% in the previous quarter. In Q2, these were as high as 3.5%. And the decline is a further testament to CSWC's credit quality, something that has remained resilient during the high interest rate environment.

Premium Valuation

As previously mentioned, the potential for additional economic weakening and lower base rates has kept me cautious on many BDCs.

However, with the way CSWC has performed, seeing improvements in their non-accruals, if I had to suggest one stock to stay invested in that I thought could navigate and avoid a potential dividend cut, Capital Southwest would be it.

However, their premium with a P/NAV ratio of 1.36x continues to keep me at a hold. Moreover, if we do see interest rate cuts this year, the sector could get cheaper as portfolio yields decline as a result.

This would likely cause further dividend coverage tightening, which could result in a potential pull back.

Furthermore, CSWC's current P/NAV ratio is currently one of the highest in the sector in comparison to some of the higher-quality peers:

Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.62x

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL): 1.10x

Ares Capital: 1.12x

So, although CSWC is attractive from an income standpoint, I suggest investors wait for a potential pullback before adding, preferably around $20 per share.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite their improvements, economic uncertainty remains high. Moreover, it's likely we could see tariffs start to show up in reports in the coming months.

And although this would likely result in the FED holding rates for longer, this could impact borrowers, causing a spike in non-performing loans as a result.

Moreover, this could also cause PIK income to increase as borrowers could see financial distress from higher for longer interest rates.

While I expect Capital Southwest to successfully navigate due to their financial position, this would likely limit upside and cause their share price to decline.

But if you're an investor currently living off income and are adamant on holding or investing in BDCs, Capital Southwest would be my suggestion.

data.title: Red Violet: Undercovered Stock But Potentially Priced
data.description:
Introduction

In my Nutanix article, I mentioned that I look for technology stocks with the following characteristics: recurring revenue, high gross margins, profitability, and expectation beats. Red Violet (NASDAQ:RDVT) is another company I find excellent on all those fundamental measures. But what's even more surprising is that the stock is simply not given much attention by Wall Street. According to FactSet, there was one analyst actively covering the stock in July and only two this month (August). This means potential future growth may be overlooked by the market, making it a great stock in the long term, given its strong fundamentals and growth prospects. As I detail later, near-term developments seem to be priced in.

Business

Industry

Red Violet primarily operates in the data analytics industry with a focus on fraud detection and prevention. Their technology focuses on providing real-time identification of people (as well as businesses and assets), so I would like to narrow my focus on the fraud detection market as the data analytics industry is quite broad.

As we can see from the chart above, the overall industry is yet to reach its exponential growth stage. As digital transactions continue to become more mainstream, costs from surging cybersecurity fraud continue to rise. This, in turn, increases the demand for preventive solutions, with the North American market projected to grow nearly 200% by 2030. Given that nearly all of Red Violet's revenue is generated in the United States, the industry backdrop is supportive of high-growth potential.

Performance

Red Violet's infrastructure, CORE, is a cloud-native AI/ML-driven platform that supports their two primary customer-facing solutions: IDI and FOREWARN. IDI is an analytics provider that helps organizations in various sectors (finance, healthcare, defense, etc.) to make data-driven decisions. The other platform, FOREWARN, which is also the more popular of the two, is an app-based solution for the real estate sector, providing background information on potential customers. As of last quarter, FOREWARN made up 97.3% of total billable customers.

So how have these services performed? Quite well. In the last six quarters, revenue and EBITDA have beaten expectations, driven by customer growth (as seen above). With a 96% revenue retention rate as of last quarter, Red Violet continues to generate recurring revenue from a sticky customer base, as pricing contracts have represented 74–78% of total revenue since Q1 2024. As time goes on, I would like to see revenue retention climb above 100% and pricing contracts represent around 80% of revenue as further proof of a strong business model. But for now, I think the revenue figures are signs of a favorable growth outlook.

Additionally, Red Violet's strong margins are a sign of steady growth. Often, adding new customers drives up costs and keeps margins stagnant, but this has not been the case so far. Gross and EBITDA margins have been improving consistently YoY. As of Q2, Red Violet reported an all-time high of 84% adjusted gross margin. Healthy margins are a sign of sustainable growth, favorable for long-term prospects.

It's also important to note that Red Violet retains a healthy balance sheet, with $38.8 million worth of cash equivalents and only $2.9 million worth of debt.

For the foreseeable future, FOREWARN continues to be the main focus for management. In their recent Q2 earnings call, management noted they wanted to invest heavily in expanding FOREWARN to new industries beginning in 2026, a logical move in my view given the strength of the platform.

Catalysts

Government Expansion

Management wants to expand into emerging markets where Red Violet currently does not have a strong penetration. This primarily includes the government vertical, an untapped source of large non-cyclical revenue. Back in March 2024, Red Violet appointed Jonathan McDonald (previously at TransUnion) to lead the public sector division and execute on this initiative. Government contracts in identity intelligence usually carry longer integration cycles and higher switching costs, enhancing customer stickiness. Management signaled this expansion to be a key driver in 2026, and I expect government contracts to help drive revenue expectation beats in future quarters.

Partnerships

Given that Red Violet is a smaller name in the IT industry, partnerships make it easier to cross-sell products and reach a larger addressable market. Back in May, Red Violet announced an integration of its IDI solution with Ping Identity to deliver no-code identity orchestration for enterprises' digital workflows, making it easier to use Red Violet's platform. Although IDI is the smaller and less popular segment of Red Violet's brand, it still holds product value since it is backed by the CORE platform. A strategic partnership allows for growth without outsized spending on marketing a less popular product, supporting additional top-line revenue growth without compressing margins. As Red Violet continues to expand into verticals and acquire more customers, I would expect to see more partnerships that would lift revenue through cross-selling in future quarters.

Valuation

The street price target, according to FactSet, is currently $56. But from a relative standpoint, Red Violet has considerably higher multiples than its peers.

Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

Given the company's high multiples, I look at the intrinsic valuation for Red Violet through a discounted cash flow analysis.

From the years 2025-2027, in my analysis, I used CapIQ street estimates for revenue growth, margins, CapEx, and D&A. I project changes in NWC as a % of sales. Given there was no data available for 2028-2029, I used my own projections to reflect my views on the company's growth prospects. I assumed a 16% revenue growth rate in 2028, 2029, a bit higher than former years as I anticipate Red Violet growing in size as it captures a larger market share. For EBITDA margin, I assume it stays steady and continues to follow a gradual but increasing trend of better margins. And for EBIT margin, I assume it grows to 17% by the end of 2029 to reflect the benefits of scale from a larger business in the future. Using these assumptions, I get an implied share price of $39.36, not far, but still below where the stock trades at.

It's important to note that technology companies often trade past their fundamental value. But in this case, I see this as a sign that near-term developments are priced in. Most of Red Violet's near-term growth prospects come from the fact that it is simply growing its revenue and nothing overly complicated (no new products, etc.). However, I do see fundamentals accelerating to where the price is justified or the price comes down near the $39 range, which would make it a good buy. However, since it trades at a high premium to peers as well, I see Red Violet as a HOLD for now.

Risks

Data Security

Given that Red Violet's products have access to enterprises' confidential data, any data breach would harm the brand image of Red Violet, making it hard to gain new customers and hurting existing client relationships. Violations of data security may also go against federal regulation, which could lead to heavy fines and impact overall profitability.

Market Concentration

Most of Red Violet's revenue is concentrated in the US. This means the company is exposed to macroeconomic and regulatory conditions that adversely affect the US, causing Red Violet to fall behind competitors who are diversified geographically.

Conclusion

Overall, Red Violet remains a great business. I would like to see the company develop a larger moat by securing more contracts, particularly with the government, in 2026. However, its current price indicates that it is not exactly the right time to buy, but future price drops (assuming sound business quality) or accelerating fundamentals would push my thesis to a buy.

data.title: Boeing Vs. Airbus: Order Strength Meets Delivery Weakness
data.description:
Boeing (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY) are facing an uphill battle to satisfy demand for airplanes. Both jet makers saw a significant increase in their stock price. At the same time, we do note that Boeing is facing a cap imposed by the FAA on production of its best-selling 737 model, and Airbus is plagued by aerospace supply chain issues. As the investment case for both jet makers is predicated on the ability to continue increasing production, it is of paramount importance to keep careful track of how orders as well as deliveries are tracking.

In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for the first seven months of 2025. As mentioned in prior analyses, we are uniquely providing value estimates on orders and deliveries to more closely reflect the actual benefit to the businesses.

Below, I have attached the explanation of the analysis methodology from a prior report:

Explanation of Analysis Methodology For Airplane Orders And Deliveries

For this analysis, I use an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value.

The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently we are not viewing things through the scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels, and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries are what matters, and that's also the area where we see that neither Boeing nor Airbus have been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view of how the recovery for both manufacturers is pacing.

Boeing Extends Lead In Airplane Order Race

In July, Boeing increased its lead on Airbus from 233 units to 250 units. This was driven by order inflow for 30 net orders for Boeing while Airbus booked three net orders. In the single aisle segment, the lead was extended by 30 units, while it shrank by three units in the wide body segment. While I believe the lead in the single aisle segment could be something that Airbus could potentially offset, the lead that Boeing has in the wide body segment is more robust and shows the company’s strength in the wide body market. The wide body tally does not include the recently announced order for 14 Boeing 777-9s by Cathay Pacific. We also note that Boeing has its net order split roughly 50:50 between single aisle and wide body jets, while for Airbus this is a 60:40 split. Typically, we do see Boeing having a 60:40 split as well, but as the company has a full order book for the Boeing 737 MAX program while production rates on the wide body programs are also improving, creating more visibility, the mix leaning somewhat more towards widebodies is something that could be expected.

In terms of value, Boeing has now logged $68.7 billion worth of orders compared to $32.2 billion for Airbus. Boeing’s lead in the single aisle market increased by $1.5 billion to $3.8 billion, while it modestly declined in the widebody market. In total, the company still has a $32.2 billion lead over Airbus.

Airbus and Boeing Book Net Airplane Orders Valued More Than $105 Billion

In the first seven months of 2025, Boeing and Airbus logged 1,060 net orders compared to 553 net orders in the comparable period last year. We note that airplane orders are up year-over-year but are still substantially below 2023 levels. In 2023, Indian airlines placed major airplane orders that shaped the year. Boeing’s share in the net orders was 62%, with the remaining 38% going to Airbus. We also note that Airbus saw a 38-unit reduction in net orders, while Boeing’s net orders stand at 3.6x last year’s comparable figure.

In terms of value, Boeing and Airbus booked $105.3 billion in net orders compared to $52.7 billion a year ago. Airbus’s net order value increased by $0.7 billion, while Boeing’s net order value increased by almost $52 billion, driven by higher net order inflow and the mix leaning more towards more expensive wide-body jets. Boeing’s net order share measured by value climbed from 32% to 65%, while Airbus’ share dropped from 68% to 35%.

So, it is safe to conclude that the sharp improvement in net orders as well as value is carried by Boeing, while Airbus saw its net order inflow increase by 10% but only 2% when measured in value. For Airbus, improving production rates could lead to follow-up orders, but for now, the substantial backlog and unpredictability of airplane deliveries prevent a substantial increase in order inflow. For Boeing, we see that improved visibility in airplane deliveries is driving new orders, while the company is also positioned extremely well to add orders as part of trade talks between the US and virtually every country in the world.

Boeing Could Lose Airplane Delivery Value Lead, Airbus Unit Lead Widens

While Boeing is in a clear leading position for the year when looking at net orders. Airbus is performing better where it matters, namely on deliveries. During the first seven months, Airbus has built an 86 unit lead in the single aisle business, while Boeing leads in the wide body segment by 41 units, bringing the total lead for Airbus to 45 units. In terms of dollar value, Boeing’s lead shrank from $2.2 billion last month to $1.3 billion. This was driven by the higher delivery volumes for the single aisle business, increasing the lead for Airbus from $3.3 billion to $4.6 billion. This was somewhat offset by higher delivery values for Boeing in the wide body segment, where the US jet maker increased its lead from $5.5 billion to $5.9 billion.

So, for Boeing to keep pace, a significant improvement in single aisle deliveries is a necessity. To me, it seems almost certain that Boeing will lose the delivery lead in the months ahead as Airbus prepares a significant increase in deliveries in the final months of the year and Boeing is unlikely to increase Boeing 737 MAX production this year while its inventory of already built airplanes is shrinking. This is somewhat offset by higher production rates on the Boeing 787 program. For Airbus, their ability to improve A320neo and A350 output is going to be pivotal. We also note that on the low-end of the single aisle market, Airbus is positioned well with the A220 program, which provided $1.8 billion in delivery value so far this year. Boeing has no competing product there. Realistically, we see that around 40% of Airbus’ lead in the single aisle dollar value is driven by the A220 program, for which Boeing has no competitive answer.

Airbus and Boeing Airplane Deliveries Top 700 Units

Boeing and Airbus have a combined delivery total of 701 airplanes in the first seven months of the year, compared to 618 deliveries a year ago. The delivery totals are not yet at 2019 levels (Boeing’s crisis year) but show a substantial improvement year-on-year. Interestingly, Airbus delivery totals trail last year’s figures by 27 units compared to 17 units a month earlier. Boeing, on the other hand, increases delivery totals by 110 units from 218 to 328 airplanes. It shows a somewhat unexpected share in deliveries: 53% for Airbus and 47% for Boeing. It looks a lot like the duopoly we have been used to.

In terms of delivery value, we see an increase from $41.6 billion to $48.4 billion. Airbus delivery value contracted from $25.5 billion to $23.6 billion while Airbus grew delivery value from $16 billion to almost $25 billion. In terms of market share, we see that Boeing is actually leading driven by higher wide body deliveries, and we see that also in terms of deliveries, the duopoly appears to have been re-established.

Going forward, I do expect that Airbus will expand its lead and actually take the lead in dollar values. However, we do note that Boeing, according to my estimates, is running around 144 airplane deliveries behind when considering the 2020-2023 trend, and Airbus is running around 113 airplanes behind. So, in the delivery ramp-up, we see that both jet makers are behind by almost 260 airplanes.

Conclusion: Boeing And Airbus Face Recovery With Hurdles

In terms of orders and order value, Boeing is still leading and has expanded its lead sequentially. For Airbus, it will not be easy to log more orders this year but it also does not have to. What Boeing and Airbus need to do is improve their deliveries. We see that in terms of deliveries, Airbus is in the lead and likely to expand that lead, but it is Boeing that is making most progress while Airbus is actually performing worse year-on-year. We see aerospace supply chain issues more pronounced in Airbus’ figures, while Boeing is showing strong improvement on a combination of higher production rates and improving production rates.

For both jet makers, the second half of the year will be a challenging one. Boeing will have to lean increasingly more on its production rates rather than deliveries from backlog and secure FAA approval to increase production with likely a material impact early next year while Airbus has to deliver on its expectations of a backloaded delivery profile.

data.title: Alphabet: Maintaining An Overweight Rating At All-Time High
data.description:
The Google parent company is trading at its all-time high and this, curiously, has nothing to do with an alleged AI bubble. On Seeking Alpha, I first covered Alphabet around a year ago. Since then, the stock is up 24%, yet still has more than reasonable valuation multiples, similar to those a year ago. With that, the “Alpha-Bet” story plays out as expected and stems from enormous fundamental strength and not multiple-expansion.

Alphabet continues to be my second-largest position, representing almost 10% portfolio weighting. I have been holding and accumulating it for several years and have been covering it publicly since January 2024. With this conviction, I am obviously far from alone, as Seeking Alpha analysts’ ratings for Alphabet are overwhelmingly bullish – with 29 analysts rating the stock Buy or Strong Buy, only 4 Hold and nobody rating Sell. Wall Street analysts’ conviction is similar.

With that, Alphabet appears to be a gem in plain sight and even despite the bullish narratives, the stock is nowhere near bubble territory. This is why Alphabet remains so interesting.

Return On Assets Robust Despite PP&E Expansion

Ever since Alphabet began its investment spree into datacenter infrastructure and AI development, worries about the return on these investments were expressed. Of course, we should acknowledge the potential risks of over-investing and an associated decline in return on assets. However, this venture is optionless, Alphabet can afford it and remains in the comfortable position of cutting Capex if AI returns would not occur as expected, immediately freeing up vast amounts of cash. Refer below for a brief asset side balance sheet check.

  1. Net PP&E has become the company’s largest asset line item accounting for 43% of Total Assets, followed by Cash And Equivalents making up 19% of Total Assets. On a positive side note, Goodwill appears to have no critical weighting in the balance sheet at only 6% of Total Assets.
  2. At an annual rate of 24%, Net PP&E is also the fastest-growing asset line-item over the past decade. For reference, Total Assets had a CAGR of 14% over this period.
  3. During that time, Net Income and Operating Cashflow grew 23% and 19% annually.
  4. As a result, Return On Assets even gradually doubled from 11% in 2015 to a record high 23% just recently. When only put into perspective with Net PP&E, profitability in relation to this line-item would have remained almost flat.

In short, this means that despite the rapid investments into building infrastructure and the surge in recorded assets, the even faster-growing bottom line supported ever-increasing return on assets.

Refer to the table below to see that all three of Seeking Alpha’s return metrics for Alphabet are far higher than the company’s own 5-year averages, despite an already lengthy period of significantly increased Capex. So far, nothing hints at reduced profitability due to these bold investments, but mid- to long-term risks remain.

Q2 Was On Track

While assessing Alphabet’s latest Q2-25 figures, I am partly referencing Q2-24 from last year’s article and the Q2-23 YoY changes back then.

Google Search maintains strong double-digit growth of 12% despite fears of AI disruption, albeit slightly slower than last year (14%). YouTube Ads remains at 13% YoY growth and Subscriptions accelerated to 20% YoY (14% last year). Google Cloud accelerated as well to 32% YoY (29% last year).

With that, revenue shares continue to shift away from Google Search, despite still growing double-digits, to Cloud. Google Search revenues accounted for 56% of the total recently (57% a year ago) and Google Cloud climbed to a revenue share of 14% (12% last year).

Operating margins remained unchanged at 40% for Google Services, after posting significant margin expansion last year. Instead, Cloud margins almost doubled to 21%.

TTM Operating Cashflow continued to climb to record highs at $134 billion recently, but due to simultaneously record-high TTM Capex of $67 billion, Free Cashflow of $67 billion is still at the same level as it was four years ago. In other words, half of the company’s Operating Cashflow is invested. Roughly 90-100% of the remaining Free Cashflow is returned to shareholders through buybacks or dividends.

Additional Thoughts And News

To round out the article, this section is dedicated to briefly shed light on aspects and news that are not the focus of this article. On the risk side, the most pressing issues remain the ongoing monopoly and regulatory lawsuits against Alphabet and their potential consequences.

On the positive side, the quality of Alphabet services continues to be apparent as even competitors seek the company’s technology and infrastructure. Reportedly, Apple is in very early discussions about Gemini potentially powering the next generation of Siri. Meta Platforms just recently entered into a deal to use the Google Cloud as Meta’s own data centers are far from sufficiently covering their own demand for years to come. With that, Alphabet is quietly making important moves to cement its top-notch position as the intelligence engine of Big Tech.

Valuation Still Beyond Reasonable

My Cost of Equity estimate for Alphabet remains unchanged at 10%, as risk-free rates have increased since last year, but the company’s beta came down notably to a value in-use very close to 1. As a result, however, Alphabet is unlikely to be exempt from temporary market turbulence and downturns, should they occur.

At a GAAP / Non-GAAP P/E of 22, Alphabet currently has an earnings yield of 4.5%, leaving a residual growth rate of 5.5% to be expected for the company. Looking at 2027 FWD P/E of 17, the FWD earnings yield is 5.9%, leaving a residual growth rate of 4.1% for Alphabet.

With that, Alphabet is almost identically valued as last year at this time, or even slightly lower. Furthermore, I added a 2% flat line discount on the Cost of Equity to additionally reflect the regulatory risks, leading to a CoE of 12%. To justify that, Alphabet would need to deliver residual growth of 7.5% as of today or 6.1% after 2027.

My conclusion remains unchanged, that all these residual growth rates appear easily achievable for Alphabet during the decades ahead. Of course, this earnings-based valuation does not directly account for the decreased Free Cashflow conversion due to high Capex in the near term. An FCF-based valuation might therefore come to a slightly more cautious conclusion, which poses another set of risks potentially causing market participants’ price-opinion about Alphabet to differ.

Takeaway

Overall, I continue to see the gradual, multiple-stable upwards drift of Alphabet stock as a gift. The company continues to be one of the most obvious Big Tech leaders yet remains spared from any bubble-like price movements. Alphabet remains one of my highest conviction long positions.

data.title: Carillon Scout Mid Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The Russell Midcap® Index’s return was positive in the second quarter despite a sharp selloff in early April following President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement. The weakness in equities, bonds and the U.S. dollar following the announcement likely convinced the president to delay collecting additional tariffs, which provided more time for trading partners to negotiate better terms. Investors sensed a standoff on tariffs, so the focus shifted to the “One Big Beautiful Bill” and deregulation, both of which investors view more favorably. Sector returns for the index were mostly higher, though the more defensive consumer staples and real estate sectors were down. The energy sector reported the worst results for the index as oil producers and service companies performed poorly despite Middle East geopolitical events in June that temporarily pushed up oil prices.

The macroeconomic environment was relatively steady, with gross domestic product (GDP) estimates bouncing back from a weak reading in the first quarter. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) remained on hold as it monitored the impact on inflation from tariffs. Unemployment remained low, though weekly unemployment claims trended higher. The employment picture looked much like the overall economy, in a standoff, as employers wait and see on fiscal and monetary policy.

Investment Team

Derek Smashey, CFA, Lead Portfolio Manager

John Indellicate, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Jason Votruba, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Eric Chenoweth, CFA, Portfolio Co-Manager

Characteristics

Total Net Assets (billions): $3.09

Number of holdings: 122

Top 10 Holdings

EQT Corp. (EQT)

Encompass Health (EHC)

Viper Energy (VNOM)

Casey’s General Stores (CASY)

ATI (ATI)

Agree Realty (ADC)

First Horizon (FHN)

Quanta Services (PWR)

CenterPoint Energy (CNP)

STAG Industrial (STAG)

Portfolio Review

Top securities

ATI produces high-performance materials for the aerospace and defense markets as well as for critical applications in electronics, medical, and specialty energy. ATI has transformed itself into a producer of high-quality nickel and titanium alloys and shed its exposure to commoditized stainless steel sheet metals, achieving an aerospace mix that generates a majority of overall revenues. Also boosting investor interest have been a rising aerospace production outlook, new business awards from a large European jet manufacturer, and improved execution of more value-added engine and airframe parts. We believe ATI should deserve to trade more in line with other aerospace parts suppliers as the company proves it can produce more consistent revenue growth at higher margins. Potential opportunities are also developing around fusion and nuclear energy.

Cloudflare (NET) provides internet infrastructure services that enhance website and application performance, security, and reliability. The stock performed well as the company showed solid progress in enterprise penetration as well as cloud security adoption. For example, Cloudflare won a record-sized deal for its Workers platform over a larger competitor, along with several multiyear large cloud security contracts during the quarter. Its financial metrics have also improved, helped by shortening sales cycles, stabilizing churn rate, and improving net-new annual recurring revenue growth. In our view, Cloudflare is one of the most competitive artificial intelligence (AI) inferencing platforms in the market, and we expect the business to grow rapidly for the next few years along with steady margin expansion.

Quanta Services provides infrastructure solutions to leading energy developers and large multistate utilities. Quanta is winning new business tied to the build out of AI power infrastructure as well as marquee renewable energy projects. The onshoring of manufacturing capacity and growth in electric vehicles are also driving increased electricity demand. There are several large transmission projects that Quanta is constructing, which we expect should provide revenue growth for years to come.

Celestica (CLS) is a contract manufacturer focused primarily on providing data center hardware components as well as aerospace and defense and industrial parts. The company reported impressive quarterly results in a highly uncertain macroeconomic environment driven primarily by strong demand for networking switches, with demand for 400-gigabyte switches being persistently strong while a ramp-up of demand for 800-gigabyte switches provided the upside. White box solutions in the networking industry are gaining share against branded vendors due largely to their lower total cost of ownership and more flexible designs. We forecast that estimates will move up throughout the year as we believe the company should continue to win new deals. In addition, we expect that the existing hyperscaler programs should ramp quicker than anticipated, which may provide further earnings upside.

GE Vernova (GEV) provides technologies and services for generating, converting, storing, and managing electricity, including gas, nuclear, wind, solar, and grid solutions. As with Quanta Services, investors see significant electricity demand growth led by AI data centers, in addition to other drivers. Led by increasing demand for gas turbines, earnings expectations continue to trend higher as GE Vernova’s backlog already stretches into the next decade. Also, as nuclear power is being reconsidered, nuclear power plant turbines and small modular reactors could add additional growth. We see significant opportunities for this market leader to support power demand growth.

Bottom securities

Viper Energy owns mineral and royalty interests in oil- and gas-producing acreage in the Permian Basin of West Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Oil prices, which drive the company’s sales, were weak during the quarter. Fears of near-term oversupply weighed on the oil market, as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) increased output. Despite the near-term concerns for oil, we think the market will regain some health when OPEC finishes its output hikes later this year. We believe Viper remains a capital-efficient and cash-generative way to invest in oil and gas.

Zimmer Biomet Holdings (ZBH), which manufactures and sells medical implants for joint replacements, lagged. Despite an overall solid earnings report, Zimmer lowered guidance due to dilution from a recent acquisition and the estimated impact from announced but not yet implemented tariffs. The company also lost some market share in its knee replacement product. We view the lowered guidance as a one-time reset that should likely reverse given the recent news around tariffs, and we think that an upcoming new release cycle should help accelerate Zimmer’s revenue growth.

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) owns and operates multifamily housing in the Sunbelt. Rent growth has been stagnant in the region, as new supply deliveries have depressed pricing. There have also been concerns about population declines in the Sunbelt from more deportations and potential legal action around how competing apartments set pricing. We think those concerns are mostly overblown and expect rent pricing to improve as the industry has already moved past the peak of new supply coming to market.

Coca-Cola (KO) Consolidated is one of the largest Coca-Cola bottlers in the United States. First-quarter earnings were a bit disappointing as higher costs related to bottled water and aluminum weighed on profit margins. Given how the company’s brands tracked at store scanners over the quarter, we expect earnings to improve for the balance of the year. Coca-Cola Consolidated is rare in that it not only distributes Coke products, but also Dr. Pepper and Dunkin Donuts products, giving it denser and less competitive routes than most peers.

Aftermarket auto parts supplier LKQ (LKQ) delivered lackluster performance. As the cost of repairing cars has increased, the number of cars that are being repaired, which is the source of demand for LKQ’s domestic operations, has declined. Bad weather in Europe and unclear messaging around the impact of tariffs also weighed on the stock following its first-quarter report. We believe the tariff fears will subside, and with management’s increased focus on execution and margin, earnings results should be more attractive going forward.

Outlook

We think risk-adjusted opportunities are balanced for the broad mid-cap indices after the recent market rally. It’s fascinating how quickly market sentiment can change. In just a few months, investor sentiment went from extreme pessimism to optimism. Crowding into more defensive, lower-volatility stocks in early April gave way to bidding up speculative stocks tied to cryptocurrencies, nonprofitable technology, and retail investor favorites in June. The quarter led off with a significant tariff scare, so the defensive positioning initially looked prescient. Trump quickly pivoted, however, postponing many of his tariffs for 90 days and essentially stopping government efficiency efforts led by the U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). We were somewhat defensively positioned early in the quarter but quickly repositioned the portfolio more aggressively when Trump announced the tariff delay. At the time, numerous stocks were oversold due to macroeconomic concerns, which presented an interesting opportunity. Now, with the Russell Midcap Index up more than 20% from the lows, much of the risk has been priced out of the market, in our view.

We see the market as a bit complacent as exhibited by the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index (VIX), which is back to February levels, and the low put-to-call ratio. Both metrics provide insight into how options investors see future volatility and risk. Some investor sentiment indicators are also pointing to optimism, such as the CNN Fear and Greed Index. We understand why the market has a sanguine outlook – inflation is coming down, which may give the Fed a chance to cut interest rates later this year; the One Big Beautiful Bill should drive fiscal expansion; and both the U.S. dollar and oil prices are moving lower. We can debate the long-term fiscal implications of these developments, but in the near term they tend to support domestic equity markets.

In many cases, stocks with good fundamentals (and in some cases just a good story) are rather fully valued. Of course, these stocks may continue to move higher if there’s no reason for capital to rotate into out-of-favor companies. Stocks that appear favorably priced based on our work tend to suffer from various headwinds, such as companies tied to weak housing fundamentals, non-digital advertising, offline retail, and renewable energy and oilfield services, among others. In these circumstances, we will remain patient as we dig for stocks with under-appreciated earnings power and long-term competitive advantages at reasonable prices.

The Carillon Scout Mid Cap Fund is avoiding significant macroeconomic calls at the start of the third quarter. We acknowledge the policy uncertainty from tariffs and geopolitical events (e.g., Iran, Ukraine), but we balance this against the potential for favorable fiscal and monetary support, which often outweighs other factors. As mentioned earlier, valuations look full for companies with good fundamentals, and fundamentals look weak for those with attractive valuations.

With this starting point, we are for now focusing less on the top-down outlook in favor of our bottom-up analysis and are sticking with themes we view as attractive. We maintained exposure to AI infrastructure holdings as the AI capital expenditure outlook is better than we expected a few months back. We have some exposure to companies offering AI applications, but phase 2 of the AI supercycle is developing slower than most expected, as AI-infused software applications, fully autonomous agents, and other products beyond chatbots remain in beta testing. Humanoid robotics is an interesting theme, and we expect some of our semiconductor and industrial companies to see significant growth from this innovation in coming years. It’s yet another demand boost for semiconductors and electricity. We see little priced in from robotics, so as this becomes a meaningful revenue source, these stocks may react positively.

We continue to see opportunities in domestic infrastructure, especially energy and electricity. Tariffs only accelerate reshoring and could lead to even more infrastructure spending. Consumer companies that are more isolated from tariff impacts, such as those that provide experiences or that provide good consumer value, also remain attractive. We see potential opportunities in retail, restaurants, and entertainment when there’s a strong consumer value proposition and significant long-term growth potential. As always, we prefer to own bottom-up, long-term compounders at attractive prices, but we must be aware of the political, economic, and monetary backdrop to protect capital and identify opportunities.

Definitions:

Aftermarket refers to parts or accessories that are not produced by an original equipment manufacturer but are designed and sold to be used with or in place of the manufacturer’s parts.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy. AI inferencing refers to an AI model producing its own predictions or conclusions or working on its own to solve a task.

Beta testing refers to the field testing of a new product with real users.

Call options are financial contracts that give the buyer of the option the right, but not the obligation, to buy a stock, bond, commodity, or other instrument – known as the underlying asset – at a specified price (the “strike price”) within a specific time period (the expiration or time to maturity). The buyer profits when the underlying asset increases in price.

Capital expenditures, or capex, are monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain physical assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

Churn refers to customers who stop doing business with a company. The churn rate is commonly expressed as a percentage, for example, of service subscribers who cancel their subscriptions during a given period.

The CNN Fear & Greed Index gauges stock market movements to try to determine whether stocks are fairly priced. It assumes that excessive fear tends to drive down share prices, and too much greed tends to have the opposite effect. It is calculated using seven indicators that measure some aspect of stock market behavior. They are market momentum, stock price strength, stock price breadth, put and call options, junk bond demand, market volatility, and safe haven demand. The index tracks how much these individual indicators deviate from their averages compared to how much they normally diverge. The index gives each indicator equal weighting in calculating a score from 0 to 100, with 100 representing maximum greediness and 0 signaling maximum fear.

Contract manufacturers produce products or parts of products for other companies to use and sell in their businesses.

Defensive positioning often is characterized by rebalancing the investment portfolio regularly to maintain an intended asset allocation. They also typically entail investing in high-quality, short-maturity bonds and blue-chip stocks, diversifying across sectors and countries, and holding cash and cash equivalents in down markets.

Defensive sectors include companies that tend to have a constant demand for their products or services, making their operations more stable during different phases of the business cycle.

Defensive stocks provide consistent dividends and stable earnings regardless of whether the overall stock market is rising or falling. Companies with shares considered to be defensive tend to have a constant demand for their products or services and thus their operations are more stable during different phases of the business cycle.

Dilution or dilutive refers to a decrease in the value of an area of an investment portfolio due to the adverse effects of influences such as portfolio allocation and security selection.

The U.S. Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) was established by an executive order that renamed the U.S. Digital Service (USDS). Its stated goal is to maximize governmental efficiency and productivity.

Enterprise penetration refers to the process of testing an organization’s computer vulnerabilities by cyber-security professionals.

Fiscal policy refers to the tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country’s economy.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is the total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one quarter or one year.

Guidance refers to statements from the managers of publicly traded companies that indicate whether they expect to realize near-term profits or losses and why.

Headwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an investment or investments.

Humanoid robotics refers to the construction of robots with human-like parts and features, such as arms, legs, torsos, and heads, as well as the ability to mimic certain human movements.

Hyperscalers refers to the largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

Liberation Day is a term used by President Donald Trump to refer to April 2, 2025, when he announced a wide range of unexpectedly high tariffs on many U.S. trading partners, triggering a global selloff of risk assets.

Macroeconomic refers to the branch of economics that focuses on seeking to understand the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Margin expansion refers to a rise in the percentage of revenue that a company earns as profit.

Monetary policy refers to the decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy’s growth, inflation, or employment levels.

Net-new annual recurring revenue refers to the net impact of new revenue gained from acquiring new customers on the annual revenues that a company receives over the course of a year.

Network switches are pieces of computer hardware that connect devices and relay packets of data to specified destinations in a computer network. Switch metrics such as 400 gigabyte and 800 gigabyte refer to the speed at which the switches are capable of transferring data.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed and signed into law in July 2025, raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, made changes to health insurance legislation, phased out or reduced credits for clean energy production or use, among other things.

Onshoring describes an effort to bring manufacturing and other services back to the United States from overseas operations.

An option is a financial instrument based on the value of underlying securities such as stocks. An options contract offers its buyers the opportunity to buy or sell — depending on the type of contract they hold — the underlying asset.

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, also known as OPEC, was founded in 1960 and is a permanent organization of 13 oil-exporting developing nations that coordinates the petroleum policies of its member countries, which are Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, Libya, the United Arab Emirates, Algeria, Nigeria, Gabon, Angola, Equatorial Guinea, and Congo.

Oversold is a term used to describe a security or group of securities believed to be trading at a level below its or their intrinsic or fair value.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Put options are financial contracts that give the buyer of the option the right, but not the obligation, to sell a specified amount of a stock, bond, commodity, or other instrument at a pre-determined price (the “strike price’) within a specified time frame. Put options increase in value as the price of the underlying asset falls, as the volatility in the price of the underlying asset increases, or as interest rates decline.

The put-to-call ratio is a measure of market sentiment that reflects whether investors expect prices to rise or fall. It is calculated by comparing the volumes of puts and calls purchased. Bearish markets feature larger numbers of puts than calls. Bullish markets feature more calls than puts.

A release cycle refers to the process of planning, developing, testing, and deploying new products, updates or features.

Reshoring describes an effort to bring manufacturing and other services back to the United States from overseas operations.

Rotation refers to the movement of investments from one industry, sector, factor, or asset class to another as market participants react to or try to anticipate the next stage of the economic cycle.

A supercycle is a sustained period of strong economic growth that drives a prolonged level of demand for the goods or services beyond what companies can deliver.

Underweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

The VIX – officially known as the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index – is a real-time market index that represents the market’s expectation of 30-day forward-looking volatility. Derived from the price inputs of the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) index options, it provides a measure of market risk and investors’ sentiments.

White box solutions refer to generic, customizable and often less expensive computer hardware or software products in place of more expensive proprietary brand name products.

Indices

The Russell Midcap® Index, the Fund’s benchmark, measures the performance of the mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000 Index.
